text: For a team below the first apron, in a trade the incoming salary may not exceed 125% of the outgoing salary plus $100,000. This is the standard salary matching rule that applies to the majority of teams in a given season.

For a team that is over the first apron, in a trade the incoming salary may not exceed 110% of the outgoing salary plus $100,000. This is a tighter matching requirement than the standard 125% rule and reduces the team's trade flexibility.

A team that is over the second apron cannot aggregate the salaries of multiple outgoing players in the same trade to acquire a single larger contract. Such a team is restricted to one-for-one player trades, or trades where the salary sent out exceeds the salary received.

A team over the second apron that completes a permitted one-for-one trade may not take back more salary than it sends out. Unlike lower tiers, there is no percentage of upside allowed for second apron teams.

A team becomes hard-capped at the first apron for the remainder of the season if it uses the non-taxpayer mid-level exception, the bi-annual exception, or acquires a player via sign-and-trade. Once hard-capped, the team cannot execute any transaction that would push its total salary above that apron threshold.

When a team trades away a player and does not immediately take back an equal salary, it may generate a trade exception equal to the departing player's salary. This exception can be used for up to one year to acquire a player of equal or lesser salary without sending money back in the same transaction.

Teams over the first apron cannot use trade exceptions generated before the previous season's trade deadline. Teams over the second apron lose access to the taxpayer mid-level exception entirely and cannot use it to supplement a trade exception.

A team that finishes a season above the second apron has its first-round draft pick seven years in the future frozen and rendered untradable until the team's salary situation improves relative to the apron thresholds.

In plain terms, salary matching exists so that a team can't simply give away a cheap player and receive a star's max contract in return. The percentage-based rules scale a team's flexibility down as its total team salary climbs into higher penalty tiers.

The aggregation ban is one of the strictest second apron penalties. In practice, it means a second-apron team cannot combine two mid-sized contracts to pursue one star-level contract in a trade, even if the total dollar amounts would otherwise satisfy matching rules.
